title: Selective Translation of ZNF281 as Part of the Integrated Stress Response System has Therapeutic Relevance for Cardio-Oncology
author: Lorenzana-Carrillo, Maria Areli
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
This repository contains the reproducible computational workflow used for RNA-seq and ChIP-seq analyses described in the accompanying Science Translational Medicine manuscript. 
 Bulk RNA sequencing data were processed using quality assessment with FastQC and MultiQC, adapter trimming with Trimmomatic, and alignment to the mm10 (mouse) or hg38 (human) reference genomes using STAR. Gene-level counts were generated using HTSeq-count. Library sizes were normalized using the trimmed mean of M-values (TMM) method, and differential expression analysis was conducted using the limma–voom framework without sample weights. Statistical inference was performed using empirical Bayes moderation with robust settings (robust = TRUE). Differentially expressed genes were defined as those with false discovery rate (FDR) ≤ 0.05 and absolute log2 fold-change ≥ 1.5. Results were visualized using MA plots. 
 ChIP-seq data were processed with adapter trimming using Trimmomatic, quality assessment with FastQC and MultiQC, alignment to mm10 or hg38 using Bowtie2, and peak calling with MACS2 (v2.1.0). Peaks were annotated to the nearest transcription start site (TSS) using BEDtools closest. Signal tracks and peak regions were visualized using IGV. 
 All original preprocessing, alignment, and peak-calling steps were performed on the Galaxy Project platform. This repository provides a portable implementation of the same analytical framework using Snakemake and Conda environments to ensure computational reproducibility and transparency. 
 This workflow enables reproduction of differential expression analysis, peak identification, peak-to-TSS assignment, and downstream integration as reported in the manuscript.
steps_raw:
This repository contains the reproducible computational workflow used for RNA-seq and ChIP-seq analyses described in the accompanying Science Translational Medicine manuscript. 
 Bulk RNA sequencing data were processed using quality assessment with FastQC and MultiQC, adapter trimming with Trimmomatic, and alignment to the mm10 (mouse) or hg38 (human) reference genomes using STAR. Gene-level counts were generated using HTSeq-count. Library sizes were normalized using the trimmed mean of M-values (TMM) method, and differential expression analysis was conducted using the limma–voom framework without sample weights. Statistical inference was performed using empirical Bayes moderation with robust settings (robust = TRUE). Differentially expressed genes were defined as those with false discovery rate (FDR) ≤ 0.05 and absolute log2 fold-change ≥ 1.5. Results were visualized using MA plots. 
 ChIP-seq data were processed with adapter trimming using Trimmomatic, quality assessment with FastQC and MultiQC, alignment to mm10 or hg38 using Bowtie2, and peak calling with MACS2 (v2.1.0). Peaks were annotated to the nearest transcription start site (TSS) using BEDtools closest. Signal tracks and peak regions were visualized using IGV. 
 All original preprocessing, alignment, and peak-calling steps were performed on the Galaxy Project platform. This repository provides a portable implementation of the same analytical framework using Snakemake and Conda environments to ensure computational reproducibility and transparency. 
 This workflow enables reproduction of differential expression analysis, peak identification, peak-to-TSS assignment, and downstream integration as reported in the manuscript.
